items.question_text: Which authorized trial-plan use covers assessing whether a requested higher altitude can be granted?
items.explanation: 13-1-3(b) authorizes trial planning to evaluate the feasibility of granting user requests. A pilot asking for a different altitude is a user request, and the trial plan is assessing whether it can be granted without creating new conflicts. This is distinct from evaluating solutions to existing predicted conflicts (a), removing a flight direction constraint (c), or removing a static restriction (d).

choices.text: Evaluating the feasibility of removing a static restriction for an aircraft.
Evaluating solutions to a predicted conflict between two aircraft.
Evaluating the feasibility of granting a user request.
Evaluating the feasibility of removing a flight direction constraint.

items.question_text: Which trial-plan category covers correcting an altitude inappropriate for direction of flight?
items.explanation: 13-1-3(c) specifically authorizes trial planning to evaluate the feasibility of removing a flight direction constraint—defined as an inappropriate altitude for direction of flight. This is a distinct category from evaluating general user requests (b), predicted conflict solutions (a), or static restriction removal (d). The example given in the paragraph is the IAFDOF situation.

choices.text: Evaluating the feasibility of removing a flight direction constraint.
Evaluating the feasibility of granting a user request.
Evaluating solutions to predicted conflicts.
Evaluating the feasibility of removing a static restriction.

items.question_text: Which Free Text Area abbreviation means a clearance was relayed through a non-ATC facility?
items.explanation: Per TBL 13-1-1, abbreviation 'C' means 'ATC clears (when clearance relayed through non-ATC facility).' This is the correct abbreviation for a clearance relayed through a non-ATC facility. 'B' means Center clearance delivered (within the ARTCC), 'N' means clearance not delivered, and 'Z' means Tower jurisdiction.

choices.text: C — ATC clears (when clearance relayed through non-ATC facility).
B — Center clearance delivered.
N — Clearance not delivered.
Z — Tower jurisdiction.

items.question_text: When control information is present, may the ACL Free Text Area be closed?
items.explanation: 13-1-8(d) requires the Free Text Area to remain open and visible when it contains control information. Closing it violates this requirement. The information must be kept visible so it is accessible for reference. Only when the information is no longer relevant may it be updated or deleted.

choices.text: Yes, the Free Text Area may be closed once the information has been entered, because the system retains the data.
No, the Free Text Area must remain open and visible when it contains control information. Closing it violates the management requirement.
Yes, but only if the information has also been relayed verbally to all affected sector positions.
No, the Free Text Area must remain open for the entire duration of the controller's shift regardless of content.

items.question_text: An ESDT overdue aircraft notification appears for a flight. The controller updates the aircraft's route of flight, and the notification is removed from the display. Has the controller satisfied all requirements for responding to the overdue aircraft notification?
items.explanation: No. While updating the aircraft's route of flight removes the ESDT overdue aircraft notification (per the note to 13-1-12), the body of the paragraph requires the controller to also take appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3, Overdue Aircraft. Removing the automation notification does not replace the required overdue aircraft procedures. The two actions serve different purposes: one clears the display indicator, the other initiates search and rescue or notification procedures.

choices.text: Yes. Updating the route of flight both removes the notification and satisfies the complete overdue aircraft response.
Yes, but only if the route update is entered within 10 minutes of the notification appearing.

No. The controller must also take the appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3, Overdue Aircraft. The route update removes the display notification but does not replace the required overdue aircraft procedures.
No. The notification can only be removed by the operations supervisor after an investigation is completed.

items.question_text: During a surveillance outage, a controller uses electronic flight data to determine whether two aircraft are maintaining the required separation. The electronic flight data appears current. What does the governing paragraph authorize electronic flight data to be used for during this outage?
items.explanation: 13-1-16 authorizes electronic flight data use during a surveillance or flight data outage only to support situational awareness while the facility transitions to alternate automation capabilities or nonradar procedures. It does not authorize using electronic flight data as a substitute for radar separation. The data supports awareness of aircraft positions and intentions during the transition—it is not a separation tool.

choices.text: To support situational awareness while the facility transitions to alternate automation capabilities or nonradar procedures. It is not authorized as a substitute for radar separation.
To provide radar separation between aircraft for the duration of the outage, because all other separation data sources are unavailable.
To serve as the primary surveillance source for separation below FL180 where reduced standards apply during outages.
To be referenced only during post-outage review; it must not be consulted while the outage is in progress.

items.question_text: Does ATOP decision support include aircraft-to-terrain separation?
items.explanation: 13-2-1(a) states ATOP provides controllers with automated decision support tools to establish, monitor and maintain separation between aircraft, and aircraft to airspace and terrain. Unlike ERAM EDST which predicts conflicts between aircraft and between aircraft and special use or designated airspace only, ATOP includes terrain separation as part of its scope. The aircraft-to-terrain separation capability is a distinguishing feature of ATOP.

choices.text: No. Terrain separation is outside ATOP's scope, which is limited to aircraft-to-aircraft and aircraft-to-airspace separation, similar to ERAM EDST.
Yes, but only if the terrain is located within special use airspace boundaries.
No. ATOP is strictly an oceanic communication system and does not provide separation decision support tools.
Yes. ATOP provides decision support tools to establish, monitor, and maintain separation between aircraft and terrain, in addition to aircraft-to-aircraft and aircraft-to-airspace separation.

items.question_text: A conflict probe red alert appears for two aircraft in ATOP oceanic airspace whose projected paths remain geometrically separate throughout the protected segment. After verifying adequate spacing using the range/bearing tool, how should the controller proceed?
items.explanation: 13-2-2(a)(3)(a)(1) authorizes overriding conflict probe when applying a separation standard not recognized by conflict probe, as listed in subparagraph a8(a) or identified by facility directive. Nonintersecting paths (8-4-2a2) is listed in a8(a). The controller may override the probe, apply the appropriate nonintersecting-paths separation standard using additional decision support tools, and must continue to ensure separation is maintained until the conflict is resolved.

choices.text: Take no action on the alert, because red conflicts must always be resolved exclusively through conflict probe guidance.

Override conflict probe and use the range/bearing tool to establish and maintain the appropriate separation standard for nonintersecting paths, while continuing to ensure separation until the conflict is resolved.
Resolve the conflict using conflict probe results exclusively, because a red alert always takes precedence over the unsupported-standard provisions.
Suppress the alert and notify the operations supervisor that conflict probe has malfunctioned for this aircraft pair.

items.question_text: Which electronic flight-strip flag identifies Mach Number Technique longitudinal separation?
items.explanation: 13-2-3(c)(1)(b) lists four reduced separation flags that must be selected appropriately. M corresponds to Mach Number Technique (MNT). R is for Reduced MNT, which is a different standard. D is Distance-based longitudinal, and W is RVSM (vertical). Each flag serves a distinct separation method and must be correctly matched.

choices.text: R — Reduced MNT, because all Mach number operations use the reduced standard.
M — Mach Number Technique (MNT).
W — RVSM, because MNT requires reduced vertical separation.
D — Distance-based longitudinal, because MNT is a longitudinal separation method.

items.question_text: What additional buffer does ATOP apply when its weather deviation tool supports RNP distance-based longitudinal separation?
items.explanation: The weather deviation tool adds an additional CDI (Converging, Diverging, and Intersecting) mileage buffer to the required separation calculated by ATOP. This buffer accounts for the navigation uncertainties when aircraft are deviating around weather. It is specific to RNP distance-based longitudinal separation applications, not radar or other separation types.

choices.text: A 10 NM lateral buffer applied in addition to the standard RNP containment value.
A time-based buffer of two minutes added to the longitudinal time standard.
A CDI (Converging, Diverging, and Intersecting) mileage buffer added to the required separation.
No additional buffer is added; the weather deviation tool only provides visual depiction of weather cells.

items.instruction: Using TBL 13-1-1 Clearance Abbreviations, select the correct abbreviation for the clearance described.
items.question_text: A controller needs to annotate that an aircraft has been cleared to the airport (its point of intended landing). Which clearance abbreviation should be used?

choices.text: F — Cleared to the fix.
A — Cleared to airport (point of intended landing).
D — Cleared to depart from the fix.
V — Cleared over the fix.

items.explanation: Per TBL 13-1-1, abbreviation 'A' means 'Cleared to airport (point of intended landing).' 'F' means cleared to a fix, 'D' means cleared to depart from a fix, and 'V' means cleared over a fix. These are all distinct clearance types with different operational meanings, and using the wrong abbreviation could mislead the receiving controller.

items.instruction: Using TBL 13-1-2 Miscellaneous Abbreviations, select the correct abbreviation for the situation described.
items.question_text: A pilot reports a TCAS Resolution Advisory during a climb. Which abbreviation should the controller use to annotate this event in the Free Text Area?

choices.text: TA — TACAN approach.
RX — Report crossing.
RA — Resolution advisory (Pilot-reported TCAS event).
RP — Report immediately upon passing.

items.explanation: Per TBL 13-1-2, 'RA' means 'Resolution advisory (Pilot-reported TCAS event).' This is the correct abbreviation for annotating a pilot-reported TCAS RA. 'TA' means TACAN approach, a completely different concept. Using 'TA' for a TCAS event would create confusion since TCAS also uses the abbreviation TA for Traffic Advisory, but the table reserves RA specifically for the pilot-reported TCAS resolution advisory event.

items.instruction: Determine which abbreviation source to consult for the control information described, and select the correct abbreviation.
items.question_text: A controller needs to annotate in the Free Text Area that an aircraft has been cleared to climb/descend at pilot's discretion. Which source should the controller consult, and what abbreviation applies?

choices.text: TBL 13-1-3 (EDST Equivalents) — 'CR' for Cruise.
TBL 13-1-1 (Clearance Abbreviations) — 'PD' for Cleared to climb/descend at pilot's discretion.
TBL 13-1-2 (Miscellaneous Abbreviations) — 'PT' for Procedure turn.
TBL 13-1-1 (Clearance Abbreviations) — 'Q' for Cleared to fly specified sectors.

items.explanation: Per TBL 13-1-1, 'PD' means 'Cleared to climb/descend at pilot's discretion.' This is a clearance abbreviation, so TBL 13-1-1 is the correct source table. TBL 13-1-2 covers miscellaneous abbreviations like approach types and pilot reports. TBL 13-1-3 covers EDST equivalents for control information symbols. Knowing which table to consult is a source-use skill—controllers must be able to quickly locate the correct abbreviation reference.

items.instruction: For each coding type, determine whether it is acknowledged on the ACL or the DL.
items.question_text: Match the automated notification coding to the correct list where it must be acknowledged: IAFDOF coding, UTM coding, and EDCT coding. Which codings are acknowledged on the ACL, and which on the DL?

choices.text: IAFDOF and UTM on the ACL; EDCT on the DL.
IAFDOF and EDCT on the ACL; UTM on the DL.
All three are acknowledged on the ACL only.
UTM and EDCT on the ACL; IAFDOF on the DL.

items.explanation: IAFDOF and UTM coding must be acknowledged on the ACL, while EDCT coding must be acknowledged on the DL. The distinction is important: the ACL handles en route flight data, while the DL handles departure-related information. EDCT relates to departure clearance times, which is why it is acknowledged on the DL rather than the ACL.

items.instruction: Determine the correct sequence of actions for handling ERT coding.
items.question_text: A sector displays ERT coding for an aircraft that it has track control for. Put the following actions in the correct operational order: (1) Initiate hand-off to the next sector. (2) Issue and send/acknowledge the route. (3) Verify the sector has track control for the flight.

choices.text: Verify track control, then issue and send/acknowledge the route, then initiate hand-off.
Initiate hand-off, then issue and send/acknowledge the route, then verify track control.
Issue and send/acknowledge the route, then initiate hand-off, then verify track control.
Verify track control, then initiate hand-off, then issue and send/acknowledge the route.

items.explanation: The first sector displaying ERT must issue and send or acknowledge the route before initiating a handoff. ERT coding must not be sent or acknowledged unless the sector has track control or the action has been otherwise coordinated. Therefore, verify track control first, address the route second, and initiate the handoff last.

items.instruction: Distinguish between what triggers or removes the ESDT overdue aircraft notification and what actions the controller must take in response.
items.question_text: An ESDT overdue aircraft notification appears. Which statement correctly distinguishes between the automation's function and the controller's required response?

choices.text: The notification is based on radar track data, and updating the route of flight will remove it. The controller must also take overdue aircraft actions per Chapter 10, Section 3.
The notification is based on the flight plan ETA, and the controller must file an amended flight plan to remove it. No further action is required.
The notification appears when the pilot reports being overdue, and the controller must immediately declare an emergency.
The notification is removed automatically after 30 minutes, and the controller's only duty is to log the event.

items.explanation: The note to 13-1-12 states the ESDT overdue aircraft notification is based on radar track data, and updating the route of flight will remove it. However, the body of the paragraph requires the controller to take the appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3, Overdue Aircraft. The automation handles the display notification; the controller handles the operational response. Both are required—the route update removes the automation indicator but does not fulfill the overdue aircraft procedural requirements.

items.instruction: Determine whether each proposed GPD use is authorized or prohibited.
items.question_text: A controller proposes three uses of the Graphic Plan Display: (1) using GPD to assess whether a reroute would avoid active SUA, (2) using GPD trajectory-based position as a substitute for radar track position, and (3) using GPD to transfer radar identification to the next sector. Which uses are authorized?

choices.text: Only use (1) is authorized. Uses (2) and (3) are explicitly prohibited.
Uses (1) and (2) are authorized. Only use (3) is prohibited.
All three uses are authorized when EDST is operational and the controller is trained on GPD.
None of the uses are authorized. GPD may only be used for post-event analysis.

items.explanation: GPD is authorized for situational awareness and strategic planning, which includes assessing SUA avoidance. It may not substitute trajectory-based positions for radar track position or transfer radar identification. Its authorized scope supports planning and awareness, not real-time separation or identification functions.

items.instruction: Compare the operational response to two different data-loss scenarios.
items.question_text: Compare the situation where forecast wind data become unavailable (13-1-14) with a situation where interfacility connectivity to an adjacent ERAM facility is lost (13-1-15). What is the critical operational difference in what data may be affected?

choices.text: For forecast wind loss, both alert and trajectory data may be affected. For connectivity loss, alert data may be affected.
There is no difference; both scenarios affect only alert data.
For forecast wind loss, only trajectory data is affected. For connectivity loss, only alert data is affected.
For forecast wind loss, only conflict probe is affected. For connectivity loss, only trial planning is affected.

items.explanation: When forecast wind data are unavailable, controllers continue using conflict probe and trial planning while recognizing that alert and trajectory data may be affected. When interfacility connectivity is lost, controllers continue using EDST while recognizing that alert data may be affected; trajectory data is not identified. Forecast winds feed the trajectory model, while connectivity loss primarily affects the sharing of alerts between facilities.

items.instruction: Determine the correct sequence of actions when modifying an Airspace Configuration Element assigned as a sector responsibility.
items.question_text: A sector team has been assigned responsibility for Airspace Configuration Elements by facility directive. The team needs to deactivate an SAA that is no longer in use. Put the required actions in the correct order: (1) Update the Airspace Configuration Element to reflect the deactivated status. (2) Notify the operations supervisor that the status has been modified.

choices.text: First update the element to reflect current status, then notify the operations supervisor of the modification.
First notify the operations supervisor and obtain approval, then update the element.
Update the element as a standalone action. Notification is required only for SAA activation, not deactivation.
The operations supervisor must perform both the update and the notification; the sector team only identifies the need for change.

items.explanation: When Airspace Configuration Elements are assigned as a sector responsibility, the sector team first updates the element to reflect current status and then notifies the operations supervisor that the status changed. Both duties are required. Updating the SAA Airspace Status View does not itself constitute coordination unless an LOA or facility directive provides otherwise.

items.instruction: Discriminate between situations where CPDLC must be primary, voice may be used, or voice must be used.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations is CPDLC required as the primary means of communication, and in which may voice be the primary method? (1) A CPDLC-connected aircraft is outside VHF coverage and CPDLC is available. (2) A CPDLC-connected aircraft is within VHF coverage and voice would provide an operational advantage. (3) CPDLC has failed and connections cannot be reestablished.

choices.text: (1) CPDLC must be primary. (2) Voice may be used for operational advantage. (3) Voice must be used because CPDLC has failed.
(1) Voice must be primary. (2) CPDLC must be primary. (3) Either may be used.
CPDLC must be primary in all three situations whenever the aircraft is CPDLC-connected.
Voice must be primary in all three situations because CPDLC is always a supplementary communication method.

items.explanation: The communication hierarchy depends on operational conditions. Outside VHF coverage, use available CPDLC as the primary means. Voice may be used when it provides an operational advantage or workload or equipment capability demands it. If CPDLC fails, use voice communications.

items.front: Delay recording: when does Hold Data Menu or Hold View automatically record a delay?
items.back: Automatically when the aircraft is cleared out of hold. No separate controller recording action is needed at that point. Manual methods require active annotation. Per 13-1-11 NOTE.

items.front: Forecast wind data unavailable: what two specific types of data may be affected?
items.back: ALERT data AND TRAJECTORY data may both be affected. This is the key distinction from a loss of interfacility connectivity (13-1-15), where only alert data may be affected. The broader impact is because forecast winds are a direct input to trajectory modeling. Per 13-1-14.

items.front: Interfacility connectivity loss vs forecast wind loss: what data is affected in each case?
items.back: Connectivity loss: ALERT data may be affected. Forecast wind loss: ALERT AND TRAJECTORY data may be affected. Both require continued use with recognition. Per 13-1-14 and 13-1-15.

items.front: Conflict probe: what two categories of factors must controllers consider when determining a solution to a conflict probe alert?
items.back: (1) Solutions involving direct routing, altitude changes, removal of a flight direction constraint, and/or removal of a static restriction for the pertinent aircraft. (2) Impact on surrounding sector traffic and complexity levels, flight efficiencies, and user preferences. Per 13-1-2(d)(1)-(2).

items.front: ATOP conflict probe: which separation standards does conflict probe NOT support? (9 categories)
items.back: Per 13-2-2(a)(8)(a): (1) Nonintersecting paths (8-4-2a2). (2) Intersecting flight paths with variable width protected airspace (8-4-2d). (3-4) Reduction of Route Protected Airspace below/above FL 240 (8-4-3a/b). (5-6) Same NAVAID: VOR/VORTAC/TACAN and NDB (8-4-4a1/a2). (7) Dead Reckoning (8-4-4c). (8) Same Direction (8-5-4). (9) VFR Climb and Descent (8-8-5).

13-2-2.items_note: Intended to supersede the existing overloaded flashcard that attempts to enumerate override conditions along with unsupported standards. This card focuses solely on the 9 unsupported separation minima for source-use reference.

items.front: Weather deviation tool: what longitudinal separation paragraphs does the tool reference for RNP distance-based applications?
items.back: FAA Order JO 7110.65 paragraphs: 8-7-3 (Longitudinal Separation), 8-8-3 (Longitudinal Separation), 8-9-3 (Longitudinal Separation), and 8-10-3 (Longitudinal Separation). Also references 13-2-2 (Conflict Detection and Resolution). Per 13-2-5 references.

items.front: ATOP automated coordination: what two exceptions prevent automated coordination from constituting complete coordination?
items.back: (a) When the aircraft is in CONFLICT with another in the receiving sector. (b) When otherwise specified in FACILITY DIRECTIVES or LOA. In all other cases, automated coordination constitutes complete coordination between ATOP sectors internally and across adjacent ATOP facilities. Per 13-2-6.
